perturbation.original_text: 2.07 Return of Confidential Information Upon the Disclosing Party’s written request, the Receiving Party shall promptly ret urn to the Disclosing Party all Confidential Information in its possession or control, and promptly destroy or erase all notes, memoranda and other material prepared by the Receiving Party which reflect, interpret, evaluate, include or are derived from any Confidential Information and the Receiving Party shall certify such destruction in writing to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.07 Return of Confidential Information Upon the Disclosing Party’s written request, the Receiving Party shall return to the Disclosing Party all Confidential Information in its possession or control within 30 days of the request. There is no obligation to destroy or erase notes, memoranda or other material prepared by the Receiving Party which reflect, interpret, evaluate, include or are derived from any Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a contradiction concerning the Receiving Party's obligations upon the Disclosing Party's request for the return of Confidential Information. The original clause states the Receiving Party must 'promptly' return all confidential information and 'promptly destroy or erase' all related notes and materials. The modified clause imposes a 30-day return timeframe and removes the obligation to destroy related materials. This creates a clear in-text contradiction within section 2.07 as to the extent of the Receiving Party's responsibilities and the time frame for fulfilling them, creating uncertainty in enforcement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.07 Return of Confidential Information Upon the Disclosing Party’s written request, the Receiving Party shall return to the Disclosing Party all Confidential Information in its possession or control within 30 days of the request. There is no obligation to destroy or erase notes, memoranda or other material prepared by the Receiving Party which reflect, interpret, evaluate, include or are derived from any Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies section 2.07 to specify a 30-day return timeframe and remove the obligation to destroy related materials. The contradicted text, which is exactly the same as the changed text, reflects these modifications. While they are identical, the explanation states that the original text required 'prompt' return and immediate destruction, which the modified text directly contradicts by allowing 30 days and removing the destruction requirement. This creates a strong in-text contradiction concerning the time frame and extent of the Receiving Party's obligations.

perturbation.original_text: 3.02 Continuing Obligations Regarding Confidentiality The obligations of confidentiality contained herein shall survive the expiration or termination of the Contract, SOW, Amendments, or RFP Terms.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.02 Continuing Obligations Regarding Confidentiality The obligations of confidentiality contained herein shall survive the expiration or termination of the Contract, SOW, Amendments, or RFP Terms for a period of one (1) year.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a contradiction by limiting the duration of the confidentiality obligations to one year after termination. This directly contradicts section 3.09, which states that the non-disclosure obligations survive for a period of 3 years. This creates an in-text contradiction regarding the length of the confidentiality period, making it unclear which term prevails.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.09 Termination This agreement continues in effect for three years. Either party may terminate t his CNDA for any reason by providing the other with 30 days’ advance written notice . The termination of this CNDA , or the completion or abandonment of the Permitted Purpose, shall not affect the survival of the non -disclosure obligations under this CNDA , which obligations shall survive for a period o f 3 years .
perturbation.justification: The changed text in section 3.02 limits the confidentiality obligations to one year after termination, while section 3.09 states that the non-disclosure obligations survive for three years. This is a direct contradiction regarding the length of the confidentiality period, creating an inconsistency as per the definition of 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: 3.09 Termination This agreement continues in effect for three years. Either party may terminate t his CNDA for any reason by providing the other with 30 days’ advance written notice . The termination of this CNDA , or the completion or abandonment of the Permitted Purpose, shall not affect the survival of the non -disclosure obligations under this CNDA , which obligations shall survive for a period o f 3 years .
perturbation.changed_text: 3.09 Termination This agreement continues in effect for one year. Either party may terminate this CNDA for any reason by providing the other with 30 days’ advance written notice. The termination of this CNDA, or the completion or abandonment of the Permitted Purpose, shall not affect the survival of the non-disclosure obligations under this CNDA, which obligations shall survive indefinitely.
perturbation.explanation: The modification introduces a contradiction by stating that the agreement is in effect for only one year, which contradicts the implied duration of the survival of non-disclosure obligations, which is stated to survive indefinitely. The original text states the agreement is in effect for three years and non-disclosure obligations survive for 3 years, which is more consistent. The change affects the in-text consistency regarding the total duration of the agreement and the survival period of its confidentiality terms.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.09 Termination This agreement continues in effect for one year. Either party may terminate this CNDA for any reason by providing the other with 30 days’ advance written notice. The termination of this CNDA, or the completion or abandonment of the Permitted Purpose, shall not affect the survival of the non-disclosure obligations under this CNDA, which obligations shall survive indefinitely.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies Section 3.09, shortening the agreement's duration to one year while simultaneously extending the survival period of non-disclosure obligations to 'indefinitely'. This creates a contradiction with the one-year agreement term, because the non-disclosure agreement is supposed to last indefinitely. The original text was more consistent as the duration of the agreement and the non-disclosure agreement were of equal duration.

perturbation.original_text: 3.11 Residuals Notwithstanding the confidentiality obligations contained in Article 2 hereof, the receiving party may, during and after the term hereof, use in its business any Residual Information. “Residual Information” means the ideas, know -how and techniques that would be retained in the unaided memory of an ordinary person skilled in the art, not CONFIDENTIALITY AND NON -DISCLOSURE AGREEMENT - MUTUAL 6 | P a g e Confidentiality and Non -Disclosure Agreement – version 1 80314 intent on appropriating the proprietary information of the disclosing party, as a result of such person’s access to, use, review, evaluation, or testing of the Confidential Information of the disclosing party for the purposes described herein. An employee's memory is unaided if the employee has not intentionally memorized the confidential information for the purpose of retaining and subsequently using or disclosing it . Nothin g in this paragraph, however, shall be deemed to grant to the receiving party a license under the disclosing party’s intellectual property rights
perturbation.changed_text: 3.11 Residuals Notwithstanding the confidentiality obligations contained in Article 2 hereof, the receiving party may, during and after the term hereof, use in its business any Residual Information. “Residual Information” means any information derived from the Confidential Information, regardless of whether it is retained in the unaided memory of an ordinary person skilled in the art. This includes intentionally memorized information. Nothing in this paragraph, however, shall be deemed to grant to the receiving party a license under the disclosing party’s intellectual property rights
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a contradiction by redefining "Residual Information" to include intentionally memorized confidential information, which directly contradicts the original definition that excludes information intentionally memorized. This affects the scope of what the Receiving Party is allowed to use after the agreement, causing uncertainty regarding the permitted use of information and creating an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.11 Residuals Notwithstanding the confidentiality obligations contained in Article 2 hereof, the receiving party may, during and after the term hereof, use in its business any Residual Information. “Residual Information” means any information derived from the Confidential Information, regardless of whether it is retained in the unaided memory of an ordinary person skilled in the art. This includes intentionally memorized information. Nothing in this paragraph, however, shall be deemed to grant to the receiving party a license under the disclosing party’s intellectual property rights
perturbation.justification: The changed text redefines "Residual Information" to include intentionally memorized confidential information, directly contradicting the original definition that excludes intentionally memorized information. This creates an in-text contradiction because the original text of 3.11 clearly states that an employee's memory is unaided if the employee has *not* intentionally memorized the confidential information, while the Changed text includes "intentionally memorized information", which directly contradicts the original section.

perturbation.original_text: 3.12 No Implied Partnership This CNDA is intended to provide for the protection of the Confidential Informatio n from unauthorized disclosure and use, and does not constitute an agreement to cooperate in a joint venture, partnership, or other arrangement, or for CP to retain Supplier in respect of any particular project. Additionally, nothing in this CNDA constitu tes a warranty by the Disclosing Party of the accuracy of any Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.12 No Implied Partnership This CNDA is intended to provide for the protection of the Confidential Information from unauthorized disclosure and use, and may constitute an agreement to cooperate in a joint venture, partnership, or other arrangement, or for CP to retain Supplier in respect of any particular project, depending on the specific circumstances and mutual agreement. Additionally, nothing in this CNDA constitutes a warranty by the Disclosing Party of the accuracy of any Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: This change creates an in-text contradiction by stating that the CNDA may constitute an agreement to cooperate, contradicting the original statement that it does not constitute such an agreement. By using the words 'may constitute' and 'depending on the specific circumstances', the provision is not clear creating confusion, which causes a contradiction in-text. This creates uncertainty about the nature of the relationship established by the CNDA.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.12 No Implied Partnership This CNDA is intended to provide for the protection of the Confidential Information from unauthorized disclosure and use, and may constitute an agreement to cooperate in a joint venture, partnership, or other arrangement, or for CP to retain Supplier in respect of any particular project, depending on the specific circumstances and mutual agreement. Additionally, nothing in this CNDA constitutes a warranty by the Disclosing Party of the accuracy of any Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces an in-text contradiction because it states the CNDA 'may constitute an agreement to cooperate,' directly conflicting with the original text's assertion that it 'does not constitute such an agreement.' The phrase 'depending on the specific circumstances and mutual agreement' adds ambiguity. Since the changed text is identical to the contradicted text, that means that the change indeed caused a contradiction.
